data.title: Core CPI expected to see renewed goods strength in December, but softer shelter inflation
data.description:
December's Consumer Price Index comes out on Tuesday, giving markets and economists fresh data on how hot inflation is running. While the December data makes progress in clearing up the fog that resulted from the government's 43-day shutdown, it will still reflect some measurement issues.

The Bureau of Labor Statistic's "zeroing out" of housing costs in October's CPI, a result of the government shutdown, will continue to play out, KPMG US Chief Economist Diane Swonk told Seeking Alpha.

The consensus expects that the December CPI will rise 0.3% M/M, up from +0.2% in November, bringing the expected year-over-year increase to 2.6%, down from 2.7% in the prior month.

Excluding food and energy, core CPI is also seen rising 0.3% M/M vs. +0.2% in November, with the Y/Y comparison showing a 2.7% increase, accelerating from 2.6% in the previous month.

Citi economist Veronica Clark is penciling in a 0.27% M/M increase in core CPI, with strength in goods prices reflecting a rebound from the downward bias inherent in November, "as price collections occurred around holiday season discounts rather than throughout the whole month." Furthermore, seasonality in airfares could increase services prices. 

"But if anything, somewhat softer shelter inflation that would be clean of measurement issues should be an encouraging sign of slowing inflation, and 0.27% as we expect may underwhelm expectations for a larger mechanical rebound," Clark wrote in a note to clients.

Evercore ISI's Stan Shipley estimate a 0.34% M/M increase in CPI and a 0.38% rise in core CPI.  "Our estimate is above consensus as the pricing survey for November was done late when prices are low from holiday sales," he explained.

The consensus of 22V Research investors anticipates core CPI to rise 2.6% or 2.8%, vs. the Bloomberg consensus of 2.7%, 22V's Dennis DeBusschere said in a note. "There is a slight bias towards a higher inflation reading," he said. "In total, 41% of investors we surveyed think core CPI will be below sell-side consensus (bbg 2.7%), 48% think above."

Some 66% of the respondents think core CPI is on a Fed-friendly glide path, that is, without a significant tightening of financial conditions or a recession, which is still near a record high for 22V surveys. "Only 2% expect a recession, the lowest in our survey history."

Meanwhile, the Cleveland Fed Nowcasts for inflation see core CPI up 0.22% M/M in December and up 2.6% Y/Y. Headline CPI is estimated to increase 0.20% M/M and 2.57% Y/Y.

Chris Lau, Investing Group Leader for DIY Value Investing, doesn't expect much of a reaction from the markets after Tuesday's inflation report. "A modest increase in December CPI will not likely change the stock market’s bullish sentiment," he wrote. "The report would need to flag a sharp rise in food and shelter prices to create a panic." 

Still, food and shelter are areas of concern. Consumer discretionary and financials are likely to react the most to the report, he said.

data.description:

data.title: International Paper is one of RBC Capital's top 2026 stock picks
data.description:
International Paper Company (IP) was added to RBC Capital Markets' Top 30 Global Ideas for 2026. Analyst Matthew McKellar and his team believe that International Paper (IP) will benefit from recent North American containerboard capacity rationalization that should allow for price increases in the first half of 2026. In addition, International Paper (IP) was highlighted as executing well on meaningful self-help initiatives, which are anticipated to drive significant EBITDA improvement by 2027.

RBC Capital has Outperform-rated International Paper (IP) slotted as a long-term holding due to its leading positions in increasingly consolidated industries. "We also like the company’s more focused capital allocation strategy across cost-reduction initiatives, reliability initiatives, and high-return capital projects that can better position the company with time," wrote McKellar. IP's (IP) transformation plan is also seen driving incremental upside. 

The firm's price target of $55 on International Paper (IP) is based on a blended ~7.75X multiple on the trend EBITDA estimate and 2026 EBITDA. 

Wall Street analysts as a group have a Buy rating on IP, while the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating is flashing Hold.

Shares of International Paper (IP) are up 7.8% for the early part of 2026. The dividend yield for new buyers of the stock is 4.3%.

data.title: Mid to low cap energy stocks with the strongest earnings momentum
data.description:
As the earnings season kicks off this week, we look at mid-to-low market cap energy stocks with strong earnings momentum, which often attract investor attention as positive analyst revisions to earnings estimates can signal improving fundamentals.

The EPS Revision rating evaluates how analysts have adjusted their earnings per share estimates for a company over recent periods, with upgrades suggesting growing confidence in a company’s profitability outlook.

The following low to mid cap energy sector stocks have earned the highest possible EPS Revision rating of A+, indicating the most favorable trajectory of analyst earnings estimate changes.

Here are the stocks based on their EPS Revision rating:

  1. American Resources (AREC) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  2. Black Stone Minerals (BSM) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  3. CrossAmerica Partners LP (CAPL) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  4. Delek US Holdings (DK) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  5. VAALCO Energy (EGY) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  6. KNOT Offshore Partners LP (KNOP) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  7. Liberty Energy (LBRT) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  8. Nordic American Tankers (NAT) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  9. Nabors Industries (NBR) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

  10. Natural Gas Services Group, Inc. (NGS) — EPS Revision Rating: A+

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: Warren told Trump Congress can cap credit card rates if 'he will fight for it'
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) said that President Donald Trump called her after a speech Monday and discussed the possibility of working together to pass cap credit card interest rates and other legislation to address low affordability.

Trump proposed late Friday a one-year cap on credit card interest rates at 10%, effective Jan. 20, noting that he will not allow Americans to be “ripped off” by credit card firms whose rates have reached 20% - 30% or more.

After delivering her speech bashing Trump's record on affordability Monday, Warren said the president called her. She told him "that Congress can pass legislation to cap credit card rates if he will actually fight for it, CBS News reported."

She also reportedly urged Trump to "get House Republicans to pass the bipartisan ROAD to Housing Act, which passed the Senate with unanimous support and would build more housing and lower costs."

Credit card and credit card issuer stocks retreated after Trump's call for the 10% rate cap; here are the top SA Quant-rated credit card issuers.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Seadrill cut to Sell at Citi, expecting disappointing 2026 EBITDA guidance
data.description:
Seadrill (SDRL) -5.3% in Monday's trading as Citi downgraded the offshore drilling contractor to Sell from Neutral with a $32 price target, trimmed from $35, and initiated a downside catalyst watch to reflect the risk of disappointing FY 2026 EBITDA guidance based largely on extended downtime on one rig in Brazil (the Carina), lower uptime on active rigs and broader softness in rates as the company books 2027 rig time during 2026.

Citi analyst Scott Gruber said his discussions with Seadrill (SDRL) management noted that Q4 is tracking as expected; regarding contract negotiations with Petrobras, flagged on the last earnings conference call, management hoped for a conclusion in Q1 which should result in at least one blend and extend.

Gruber said the weak macro backdrop compels him to extend downtime on the Carina into Q1 2027 and cut operational uptime on other rigs in Brazil, and although a restart of the Capella offers a potential positive catalyst, the analyst said he already had assumed this would occur in mid-2026; as a result, he reduced his FW 2026 EBITDA forecast by ~20% to $280M, below consensus at $386M, while also lowering FY 2027 EBITDA forecast by ~14% to $492M, below
consensus at $551M.

The analyst also rates Seadrill (SDRL) as High Risk, citing drilling rig dayrates that remain volatile and continuing risk on contract renewals.

data.title: First Watch gains after saying at ICR that its restaurant pipeline is strong
data.description:
First Watch Restaurant Group (FWRG) rallied on Monday after providing a business update at the ICR Conference. 

The restaurant company said Q4 same-restaurant sales were up +3.1% and full-year same-restaurant sales rose 3.6%. Same-restaurant growth was down 1.9% in Q4, but was 0.5% higher for the full year. On the development front, First Watch (FWRG) highlighted that it had 13 new system-wide restaurant openings in Q4, consisting of 12 company-owned restaurants and one franchise-owned restaurant. For the full year, there were 64 system-wide new restaurant openings (55 company-owned and nine franchise-owned) and three closures. As of December 28, First Watch (FWRG) had 633 system-wide restaurants, consisting of 560 company-owned restaurants and 73 franchise-owned restaurants across 32 states.

CEO Chris Tomasso noted that the 2024 and 2025 new restaurant classes combined continue to outperform the comparable restaurant base and the company's own underwriting expectations. Tomasso noted that the pipeline for 2026 is strong. "We remain confident in the continued execution of our long-term strategy and extending our lead in daytime dining," added.

Shares of First Watch (FRWG) were up 3.3% in afternoon trading.

data.title: SA Asks: Does Powell DOJ probe mark end of Fed independence? (Update)
data.description:
Does the announcement of a Justice Department probe into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell following months of pressure by the Trump administration to lower interest rates mark the end of the central bank's independence?

Seeking Alpha analysts Sagar Agarwal, APAC Investment News, Geneva Investor, Eugenio Catone, and Julia Ostian weigh in. 

Sagar Agarwal: Challenging the Fed’s independence is easier said than done, primarily because the Fed’s policy actions are more public than ever before. The implication is that if the Fed were to act in a manner incongruent with prevailing economic conditions, market participants would interpret this as a loss of Fed credibility, triggering negative price reactions across all asset classes and possibly pushing the global economy into a deep and severe recession. Such an outcome would be detrimental to the ambitions of any political figure who may have orchestrated a closer coupling between the Fed and the government.

Additionally, while all seven Fed board governors, including the chair, are appointed by the sitting president of the U.S., the remaining five votes belong to the president of the New York Fed and four other regional Fed presidents selected through a rotation system. If these five voters were to believe that the Fed’s independence was being challenged, they could be publicly vocal about it—much as Chair Powell was this Sunday. 

APAC Investment News: Unfortunately, I don't believe Federal Reserve independence is especially high on President Trump's to-care-about list. I believe he will erode as much independence as possible in pursuit of his goals. Ultimately, the president's limits will likely come down to what Congress and/or the Supreme Court decide to pursue.

Geneva Investor: I don't think we are seeing the end of the Fed's independence just yet. On the contrary, I see this as Powell's last attempt at safeguarding the Fed's independence by putting his reputation on the line. However, with Powell having only four months left as Fed chair and mounting political pressure, this may not matter. Markets may be already starting to price in a "new" Fed, which, even if not fully dependent on the government, may become more easily swayed.

Eugenio Catone: In my opinion, the federal investigation into Jerome Powell is a concerning event that could set a dangerous precedent for the U.S. economy. If the central bank is no longer independent, this would mean not acting in the best interest of the country but instead favoring short-term political agendas. 

Therefore, the U.S. dollar could depreciate even further, and the 10Y bond yield could surge, despite a lower Fed Funds Rate. This divergence between the short end and the long end of the yield curve reflects all the doubts of bond vigilantes. For a net importer country such as the U.S., a further weakening of the U.S. dollar could mean upward pressure on the inflation rate.

Julia Ostian: I view this more as political theater than a structural collapse of the Fed. The Federal Reserve has survived intense political pressure before, and its independence remains intact. While headlines generate volatility, I believe the institution will continue to prioritize data over politics, though the noise level will undeniably make their communication job harder.

The biggest risk, in my opinion, is psychological. The market is quick to judge, so if investors perceive political pressure behind rate decisions, we could see inflation expectations unanchor and yields spike regardless of the Fed's actual intent.

(Updated to add commentary by Julia Ostian.)

data.title: Anthropic unveils new Cowork tool to make Claude Code more accessible
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) unveiled its new Cowork tool on Monday, in an effort to make its Claude Code coding tool more accessible.

Available right now in research preview, Cowork is available to subscribers of Anthropic's Claude Max via its macOS app, Anthropic said. The tool allows users to point to a specific folder that Claude will be able to read or edit via a chat interface. It should allow for coding tasks to be done as simply as having a chat with coworkers, Anthropic added.

“In Cowork, Claude completes work like this with much more agency than you’d see in a regular conversation,” the company said in a release, accompanying the news. “Once you’ve set it a task, Claude will make a plan and steadily complete it, while looping you in on what it’s up to. If you’ve used Claude Code, this will feel familiar—Cowork is built on the very same foundations. This means Cowork can take on many of the same tasks that Claude Code can handle, but in a more approachable form for non-coding tasks.”

Claude Code was launched in November 2024, and it has become one of Anthropic's more successful product launches. Anthropic is on the verge of raising $10B in a new funding round that would value the generative AI company at $350B.

data.title: Revolution Medicines declines to comment on M&A speculation at conference
data.description:
• Revolution Medicines (RVMD) declined to comment on recent M&A speculation at the JPMorgan Healthcare conference in San Francisco.
  • Revolution Medicines (RVMD) cited a company policy of not commenting on rumors or speculation at the conference on Monday.
  • The no comments come after reports last week that Merck (MRK) was in talks to purchase Revolution (RVMD). The WSJ reported on Friday that Merck was in discussions to acquire the cancer-drug biotech for about $30 billion.  A deal for Revolution could come as soon as later this month. Merck was discussing a deal valued between $28 billion and $32 billion.
  • Shares of Revolution (RVMD) edged lower by 0.5% on Monday after surging 50% last week on deal speculation.
